text: The spleen is an organ located in the upper left of the abdomen underneath the ribs. Its function is to filter the blood and remove any old red blood cells. It also has some immunologic function. Occasionally, people can develop problems with the spleen that cause it to not work correctly. When this happens, we sometimes recommend removing the spleen. This is through a procedure called a splenectomy. This video is of a real splenectomy performed at TriHealth. In this video, the splenectomy is being performed in a minimally invasive fashion using robotic technology. This video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical splenectomy. The purpose of this video is to give patients an idea of how the surgery is actually performed. If you are scheduled to have this procedure, your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to lift the stomach, exposing the omentum and short gastric vessels. The vessel sealer cauterizes and divides these vessels to prevent bleeding. The stomach is separated from the spleen, which is visible in the right corner of the screen, while the stomach is on the left. The spleen is then detached from the diaphragm at the superior pole, completing its mobilization.

The surgeon completes dissection at the superior pole of the spleen and proceeds to the inferior pole. The splenic flexure of the transverse colon is detached from the abdominal wall to improve access and visibility. The surgeon divides the splenocolic ligament at the inferior pole using a vessel sealer, ensuring safety despite the absence of named vessels.

The surgeon retracts the spleen medially towards the middle of the abdomen, creating tension to visualize the attachments between the lateral portion of the spleen and the abdominal wall. Using a hook electrocautery device, the surgeon divides these attachments, aiming to mobilize the spleen further by cutting through an avascular plane.

The surgeon lifts the left lateral segment of the liver to access the spleen, which is now fully mobilized except for attachments at the hilum. Using a stapler, the surgeon divides the splenic artery and vein, ensuring to avoid the tail of the pancreas. After multiple stapler firings, the spleen is freed and placed into a bag for removal from the abdomen, completing the robotic splenectomy.
